file_name: Aloupogianni et al. - 2022 - Hyperspectral imaging for tumor segmentation on pigmented skin lesions.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning and deep learning models for tumor segmentation of pigmented skin lesions using hyperspectral imaging. Specific methods included Support Vector Machines (SVM), K-means clustering with Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM), a three-dimensional Xception-based network, custom ResNet models, and a 3D CNN.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the preparation of a hyperspectral imaging (HSI) dataset of 28 pigmented skin lesions (PSL) and the proposal of two new frameworks for tumor segmentation. It details specific methods including machine learning (SVM, K-means) and deep learning models (ResNet, 3D CNN, 3D Xception), reports quantitative results from leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV) with metrics like Jaccard coefficient and AUC, and concludes with findings on performance and limitations. The abstract explicitly states the aim is to 'propose a framework' and the results section provides new empirical data, distinguishing it from a review.

title: Hyperspectral imaging for tumor segmentation on pigmented skin lesions
brief_summary: This study proposes a framework for tumor segmentation of pigmented skin lesions (PSL) using hyperspectral imaging (HSI) to assist in gross pathology. An HSI dataset of 28 PSL samples was prepared, and two segmentation frameworks utilizing machine learning (SVM, K-means with SAM) and deep learning (ResNet, 3D CNN, 3D Xception) were evaluated. Performance was assessed using leave-one-out cross-validation and testing on an unknown dataset, with metrics including the Jaccard coefficient and expert pathologist review. The results indicated that pixel-wise processing and spatio-spectral features improved segmentation, with the 3D Xception network achieving performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods while providing detailed tumor border detection. Although good performance was observed for melanocytic lesions, margins were difficult to detect in some basal cell carcinoma cases, and the masks were deemed insufficient for immediate computer-assisted diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the dataset with 12 samples (malignant). Discussed as having difficult margin detection in some cases.
Included in the dataset with 1 sample (malignant). Good performance achieved for melanocytic lesions.
Included in the dataset with 1 sample (malignant).
Included in the dataset with 2 samples (precancer).
Included in the dataset with 9 samples (benign). Closely associated with Malignant Melanoma.
Included in the dataset with 1 sample (benign). Present in the testing dataset but not in the validation/training dataset.
Included in the dataset with 1 sample (benign).
Included in the dataset with 1 sample (benign neoplasia). Had the worst prediction in terms of Jaccard coefficient.
Mentioned as a sample that was not used for the study because it had a very different structure compared to the rest of the PSL.

all_task_types.target: 2D binary masks distinguishing tumor tissue from healthy tissue/background in hyperspectral imaging data.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'tumor segmentation' and 'margin detection'. Models predict pixel-wise or patch-wise binary masks (tumor vs. non-tumor). Evaluation metrics include Jaccard coefficient (intersection-over-union), sensitivity, and specificity, which are standard for segmentation tasks. The output is described as 'predicted value PR' and 'ground truth value GT' as 2D binary masks.

datasets_used.name: Custom Hyperspectral Imaging (HSI) Dataset of Pigmented Skin Lesions
datasets_used.description: A custom-collected dataset of ex-vivo pigmented skin lesions (PSL) imaged using a hyperspectral imaging system. The dataset includes HSI data cubes, 2D tumor margin masks derived from histological evaluation by expert pathologists, disease names, and malignancy status. It was collected from patients at Saitama Medical University.

use.training: Used for training models via leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV) with 19 folds. Specifically, 'data split 2' (19 samples) was used for LOOCV, where 18 folds were used for training in each iteration. Additionally, the entire validation dataset was used to train models for final testing on the unknown testing set.
use.validation: Used for validation during the LOOCV process (evaluating on the held-out fold). Also used as the training set for the final model before testing on the unknown dataset.
use.testing: A separate set of 283,206 spectral signatures (referred to as 'data split 3' or 'unknown testing dataset') was used for final testing and visual confirmation. This set included samples such as BCC, Bowen’s disease, Melanocytic Nevus, and Blue Nevus.
use.other: Four additional samples (Paget’s disease, two Melanocytic Nevi, one BCC) were collected but excluded from the analysis due to structural differences, heavy staining, or difficulty in creating label masks.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Receiver operator characteristics (ROC) area under the curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of segmentation models by iteratively training on 18 folds and testing on the remaining fold, with average performance derived from each iteration.
To optimize hyperparameters (kernel, box constraint, and kernel scale) for the Support Vector Machine (SVM) model.
To optimize hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate, exponential decay, loss function) for the deep learning models.
To visualize and reduce the dimensionality of the high-dimensional hyperspectral data space for exploratory analysis.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver operator characteristics (ROC) area under the curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the balance between sensitivity and specificity for classification and segmentation models.
To serve as the main performance metric for segmentation, providing a balance between false positives and false negatives.
To report the summary statistics of evaluation metrics across all folds of the LOOCV process.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (average and standard deviation) across leave-one-out cross-validation folds and compared qualitatively or via metric values (Jaccard coefficient, AUC, etc.) without statistical significance testing.

LOOCV (Without Denoising).dataset_description: Data split 2 (19 folds), excluding one sample per fold for testing
LOOCV (Without Denoising).notes: Values are reported as an average across all folds. Denoising did not improve performance for pixel-wise models. Signature + SVM achieved the highest AUC (0.996) and JC (50.17) among pixel-wise models. Pretrained-Resnet had the highest accuracy (87.24) among deep learning models in this setting.

LOOCV (With Denoising).dataset_description: Data split 2 (19 folds), with spatial denoising applied
LOOCV (With Denoising).notes: Values are reported as an average across all folds. Spatial denoising improved JC performance for patch-based models but at a cost of specificity. Pretrained-Resnet achieved the highest accuracy (88.81) and sensitivity (71.62) in this setting.

Testing (Data Split 3).dataset_description: Unknown testing dataset containing BCC x5, Bowen’s disease x1, Melanocytic Nevus x2, Blue Nevus x1

3D CNN.reason: Falsely detected skin instead of tumor during validation

Kmeans.notes: Failed to detect any tumor in four cases; detected illumination artifacts

Abundance + SVM.notes: Detected homogeneous tumor masks; suffered from increased false positives in BCC and melanoma cases

Signature + SVM.notes: Detected general area of tumor; margins not delineated well; false positives in non-neoplastic areas

Pretrained-Resnet.notes: Provided clearer tumor masks for BCC samples compared to SVM; produced larger tumor masks with tendency for high false positives; labeled entire patches as tumor or non-neoplasia

Custom-Resnet.notes: Provided clearer tumor masks for BCC samples compared to SVM

3D Xception.notes: Produced more curvy tumor segments; controlled false positives compared to Pretrained-Resnet; falsely detected non-neoplastic edge at melanoma sample

Testing (Data Split 3).notes: Performance fluctuates depending on pathology. Exceptionally good performance for melanocytic PSL. SVM-based pixel-wise masks deemed closer to expected ground truth by expert pathologists despite false positives. Deep learning models provided clearer masks for BCC. Consensus: tumor masks were not sufficient for computer-assisted diagnosis.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple models: Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-means clustering with Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM), 3D Xception-based network, Custom ResNet, Pretrained ResNet, and 3D CNN.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained ResNet: Weights trained on the ImageNet dataset; only the input convolution weights were trained, while the rest of the parameters were frozen. Custom ResNet: Trained from scratch. 3D Xception and 3D CNN: Trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: 32 × 32 × 311 patches (for deep learning models). SVM models used single-pixel spectral signatures.

3D_Xception.structure: Three Xception blocks. Preserves 3D structure in the decoder. Bottleneck layer drops spectral dimension using max function. Dropout layers with 40% rate at the end of each Xception block.
3D_Xception.kernel_sizes: (3, 3, 10) for the first block; (3, 3, 20) for the last two blocks.

ResNet.Pretrained_Config: ResNet34 with an additional convolutional layer before the input to reduce 311 spectral channels to 3.
ResNet.Custom_Config: Input dimensions passed as parameter; spectral dimension reduced with 2D convolutions; all layers trained from scratch.

SVM.kernel: Radial Basis Function (RBF) with box constraint 44.184 and kernel scale 4.0136 (optimized via Bayesian optimization).
SVM.input_features: Abundance maps or Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM) signatures.

Kmeans.method: Sum of SAM similarity per pixel in the cluster.

training_details.optimizer: RMSProp (best performing combination for deep learning models).
training_details.loss_function: Binary Cross Entropy + Jaccard Coefficient loss (BCE + JC).

hyperparameter_optimization.Deep_Learning: Grid search (Optimizer: Adam/RMSProp; Learning Rate: 10^-3 to 10^-6; Exponential Decay: none/10^-5/10^-6; Loss: BCE/BCE+JC).

training_details.data_augmentation: Fourfold augmentation by vertical, horizontal, and mutual flipping of each channel of the data cube and labels. Resulted in 1780 HSI cubes for the validation dataset.

classification_or_output_head.output: 2D binary masks (tumor vs. non-tumor) for each patch or pixel.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: K-means clustering with Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM)
preprocessing_techniques: Normalized reflectance recovery from target, white reference, and dark signal HSI pixel intensities
Spatial denoising using the nonlocal meets global (NGMeet) algorithm
Foreground mask creation using iterative clustering and morphological operators to exclude background pixels
Setting background pixels to a flat zero spectrum
Patch extraction: splitting data cubes into 32 × 32 × 311 patches
Discarding patches containing no tissue pixels or large portions of skin dyes
Data augmentation: fourfold augmentation by vertical, horizontal, and mutual flipping of each channel of the data cube and labels

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Fourfold augmentation by vertical, horizontal, and mutual flipping of each channel of the data cube and the labels.

conclusions: Pixel-wise processing achieves higher segmentation performance in terms of the Jaccard coefficient compared to patch-based methods.
The simultaneous use of spatio-spectral features produces more comprehensive tumor masks.
A three-dimensional Xception-based network achieved performance similar to state-of-the-art networks while allowing for more detailed detection of the tumor border.
Good performance was achieved for melanocytic lesions.
Margins were difficult to detect in some cases of basal cell carcinoma.

The proposed frameworks require further improvement for robustness against different pathologies and detailed delineation of tissue margins to facilitate computer-assisted diagnosis during gross pathology.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a very small dataset size (28 pigmented skin lesions), which restricts the generalizability of the deep learning models and increases the risk of overfitting despite the use of leave-one-out cross-validation.

The ground truth labels are derived from histological cross-sections of ex-vivo tissue, which may not perfectly align with the surface spectral signatures captured by hyperspectral imaging, potentially introducing label noise and affecting metric calculation.

Performance varies significantly by pathology; while melanocytic lesions showed good segmentation, basal cell carcinoma (BCC) margins were difficult to detect, and specific cases like Melanophage aggregate yielded the worst predictions.

The proposed frameworks were deemed insufficient for immediate computer-assisted diagnosis by expert pathologists, indicating that current performance levels do not yet meet clinical standards for robustness and detailed margin delineation.

The testing dataset included a Blue Nevus sample not present in the training or validation sets, highlighting a potential bias in the training data distribution and raising concerns about the model's ability to generalize to unseen pathologies.

Spatial denoising (NGMeet) did not improve performance for pixel-wise models and even reduced it for patch-based models by discarding useful spectral components, suggesting that denoising strategies must be carefully tailored to the specific segmentation approach.

The study is a proof-of-concept work with minimal optimization of models (e.g., SVM trained on only 10% of signatures) to avoid long training durations, which may underestimate the potential performance of fully optimized models.

There is a risk of data leakage or misalignment between the spectral data and the histological ground truth, particularly in cases where the mapping of histological ground truth was not successful or where tissue structure differed significantly from the cross-section.

The reliance on Jaccard coefficient and visual confirmation is appropriate for segmentation, but the lack of formal statistical hypothesis testing limits the ability to claim significant differences between the evaluated methods.
